paragraphs.text: Based on the SRD-46 results I could actually retrieve, the **best amino-acid ligand for selectively complexing Cu²⁺ over Ni²⁺ at pH 7 is histidine** (SRD-46 ligand entry: **L-2-Amino-3-(4-imidazolyl)propionic acid**, i.e. histidine).

claims.text: Based on the SRD-46 results I could actually retrieve,
**best amino-acid ligand for selectively complexing Cu²⁺ over Ni²⁺ at pH 7 is histidine**

paragraphs.text:
| Ligand | SRD-46 evidence relevant to Cu²⁺ vs Ni²⁺ | pH 7 implication | Selectivity call |
|---|---|---|---|
| Histidine | Both **Cu²⁺** and **Ni²⁺** entries are present for the same ligand (**ligand_5898**); histidine also appeared in the pKa search with a neutral-pH-relevant donor set | At pH 7, histidine offers **carboxylate + amine + imidazole**, a donor combination that strongly favors Cu²⁺ | **Best supported choice** |
| Glycine / alanine / serine / Asp / Glu family | These were discussed in pKa results as mostly simple O/N donors without a strong Cu-favoring side chain | Weaker Cu/Ni discrimination near neutral pH | Inferior |
| Cysteine-type ligands | Thiol chemistry can complicate Cu²⁺ speciation/redox near pH 7 | Less clean for selective Cu²⁺ vs Ni²⁺ capture | Not preferred |

paragraphs.text: A key limitation is that the available SRD-46 tool outputs here did **not expose matched numerical Cu and Ni logβ values** for the same simple amino acids under identical conditions, so I cannot give a defensible numeric selectivity ratio from the retrieved records alone. Still, among the amino-acid ligands actually supported by the database hits, **histidine is the strongest Cu²⁺-selective option**.
